title: Whitman Ipsum
text:
I celebrate the wide expanse of the morning, the trembling birth of light upon the hills, the wind that whispers its own name through the bending grass. I stand with open hands and feel the pulse of the world beating through me — a rhythm older than memory, broader than speech. In the quiet glow of dawn, I find not a beginning, but the endless continuation of all things.

I am the sea and the sailor, the wave that rises and falls, the salt that burns and heals. My body is the shore upon which the tide of existence breaks — ceaseless, certain, alive. O vastness, O breathing wilderness of being, how you summon me to listen without fear!

I walk among crowds, yet I am alone, and in that aloneness I am joined to every soul that breathes. The child’s laughter, the laborer’s sweat, the widow’s sigh — they are all my own, and I am theirs. The city hums, the fields rustle, and I, in my walking, belong to it all.

The leaves whisper of mortality, yet they fall with grace, without protest, into the soil that birthed them. I too shall fall, and in falling, become again the dust from which stars are made. This is no sorrow, but a sacred exchange, the oldest covenant between earth and sky.

I do not shrink from the vast or the humble. The grain of sand upon my finger is as holy as the planet that spins unseen beyond the clouds. I find divinity in the footprint, in the whisper, in the unspoken tenderness between strangers on a crowded street.

Let the rivers run their course, for so must we — winding, curving, eroding and renewing. The current is not cruel, it is truth in motion. Every stone beneath the water’s skin has been shaped by the same patient touch of time.

I hear America singing, not in harmony but in the grand discord of its many voices. The farmer, the mother, the soldier, the poet — all contribute to the great unending song. I add my verse, rough-edged and unpolished, yet honest in its yearning.

I have learned to love the unnameable, the silence between words, the spaces where meaning hides. The soul, I think, is not a thing to be found, but a field to be tended. Let it grow wild, let it breathe, let it stretch toward the infinite horizon.

The night comes softly, laying its cool hands upon the day’s fevered brow. Stars gather like shepherds of thought, leading me toward wonder. The darkness is not absence but fullness — a womb of rest where tomorrow stirs unseen.

I have kissed the face of grief and found there a strange sweetness. Sorrow, too, has its music, low and steady, teaching the heart to widen. I will not resist its touch; I will let it carve me into something deeper.

O humanity, ever striving, ever stumbling! How beautiful you are in your imperfection. You build and break, love and lose, yet still you rise, trembling and defiant, reaching toward the light that forever recedes.

There are no small lives, no wasted days. Even the beggar’s breath, even the widow’s prayer, even the laughter of the lost child — all ripple outward into eternity. Nothing is forgotten; everything belongs.

I am the open road, the long horizon where earth kisses sky. Every traveler I meet is my reflection, every story a mirror of my own. Together we wander, unafraid, toward that vast and radiant unknown.

Let me be simple — the bare feet on cool soil, the sweat upon the brow, the hand extended in welcome. The soul requires little more than truth, and the body asks only to be seen as sacred in its labor.

In the end, there is no end — only continuation, only breath upon breath, wave upon wave. I have no desire to conquer the world, only to know it fully. I lay myself open to it, and in doing so, I become endless.

Love is the thread that binds the stars to the hearts of men. It is the unseen hand that guides the leaf to its fall, the mother to her song, the stranger to his kindness. Without it, all light dims, all meaning fades.

The road stretches on, beyond the edge of thought, into the country where the soul remembers its name. I walk it gladly, barefoot, unburdened. Each step is a prayer, each breath an offering.

There are miracles in the ordinary — the bread shared at dusk, the child’s question at dawn, the sigh of the wind through the shutters. Do not look to heaven for wonders; look instead to your own beating heart.

I have seen the future in the eyes of the living — not a promise of perfection, but of persistence. The world will stumble, rise, stumble again, and still it will go on. That is its grace: to fall endlessly and still continue to bloom.
